title.en: A History of Political Islam and the West – Part 3: Iran Revolution (1)
title.fa: تاریخ اسلام سیاسی و غرب – قسمت ۳: انقلاب ایران (۱)
title.th: ประวัติศาสตร์อิสลามการเมืองและตะวันตก – ตอนที่ ๓: การปฏิวัติอิหร่าน (๑)

text.en: In 1979, political Islam became headline news in the west with the Iranian revolution, which brought a 76 year-old cleric named Ruhollah Khomeini to power. Before this, the rise of Islam as a factor in geopolitics was acknowledged only by those with a specialist interest in the region; afterwards, there could be no ignoring it.

In retrospect, one of the most surprising things about it was the extent to which it surprised its contemporaries. In 1978, a CIA report on Iran stated that ‘the shah will be an active participant in Iranian life well into the 1980s’ and that ‘there will be no radical change in Iranian political behaviour in the near future.’ Iran, it was confidently asserted, ‘is not in a revolutionary or even a pre-revolutionary situation.’

Within a year, however, a 2,500 year-old monarchy had been overthrown and an Islamic republic instituted in its place. The latter fact confounded expectations that, if there was to be change, it would come from the progressive forces of the left rather than the clerics or ullama.

caption.en: The Shah, Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, in modest casual attire.

text.en: To understand how his deposition came to this pass, it is important to note that Iran had not been an autocracy from time immemorial. It is often noted that the fall of the Shah ended a 2,500 year-old institution, but the Pahlavi dynasty were blow-ins, only recently founded by an Iranian army officer, Reza Shah Pahlavi, in 1925.

caption.en: A picture of Mosaddegh as a child, standing next to an empty chair, meeting the Shah on his appointment as prime minister, and later in life under house arrest.
The plot was masterminded by the head of the CIA, Alan Dulles (far right) and co-ordinated on the ground by Kermit Roosevelt Jnr. (middle), a grandson of former president Theodore Roosevelt.
Ruhollah Khomeini in 1964 and later in life, sometime in the 1970s.

text.en: In 1964, Khomeini led a protest that centered around two bills being passed through parliament at the Shah’s behest-one, a $200 million loan from the United States, and another (on which it was clearly contingent) new law granting American personnel in the country immunity from prosecution in Iranian courts.

caption.en: Archival footage of the 1979 Iranian Revolution.
The opulent celebrations at Persepolis in 1971 to commemorate 2500 years of Iran's monarchy.
A 1970s advertisement for Rayovac, showing the western culture on display.
Demonstrators during the revolution carry pictures of Shariati (front), as well as Mossadegh (behind).

text.en: Events moved fast in early 1979. In January, the Shah made one last push to placate the opposition, by appointing a new prime minister, Shapour Bakhtiar, who was a member of Mossadegh’s National Front. Sweeping reforms, including the overhaul of SAVAK and trial of its officials, were announced, but all of this was too late.

caption.en: The Shah and his family leaving Iran on January 16, 1979.
News report from the time showing Khomeini's return to Tehran.
